text: Hello friends, this is a case of laparoscopic adhesiolysis for small bowel adhesion. This patient has repeated episode of sub-acute obstruction. For that he has admitted many times in the hospital. Actually, there is a history of laparoscopic appendectomy open. And after that we can see the serosa of the adhesion is completely adhered.

The surgeon uses cold scissors to create a preperitoneal space, carefully dissecting the peritoneum from the small bowel to establish a clear plane for further dissection. The harmonic device is then used to separate the peritoneum along with the small bowel, aiming to prevent recurrent sub-acute obstructions. Minor bleeding is observed during the initial dissection.

The surgeon identifies a clear plane and begins adhesiolysis using a harmonic scalpel. A loop of the small bowel is visibly incarcerated into the abdominal wall, contributing to repeated sub-acute obstructions and abdominal pain.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to carefully dissect the serosal adhesions of the small bowel from the abdominal wall, maintaining a safe plane to minimize the risk of perforation. Atraumatic graspers provide gentle counter traction to avoid lacerating the bowel, while a portion of the peritoneum is taken along with the serosa to prevent injury.
The surgeon identifies a good plane of dissection and prepares to apply Interceed, a re-oxygenated cellulose barrier, to the raw area to prevent re-adhesion.

The surgeon inspects the surgical area for any perforations by pressing and stretching the distal and proximal loops of the small bowel. The adhered part of the abdominal wall is carefully manipulated to check for fecal matter leakage, confirming no perforation is present. An interseed is placed over the raw area to prevent future adhesions.

The surgeon applies pressure to the manipulated bowel to check for any perforation, confirming that there is none. A piece of Interceed is placed over the raw area to prevent adhesions, covering the entire exposed section.

The surgeon places a piece of Interceed over the raw area to prevent re-adhesion. Initially, the Interceed appears solid and sticks to the tissue, but once moistened, it secretes a jelly-like substance that acts as a mechanical adhesive barrier, preventing adhesion between bowel loops.
